elements.sent-2411-001: We present a catalog of 35 new candidate (13 high confidence) isolated, young stellar systems within the Virgo galaxy cluster identified through a citizen science search of public optical and ultraviolet imaging.
elements.sent-2411-002: Only six blue blobs were known previously and here we confirm an additional six of our candidates through velocity and metallicity measurements from follow-up optical spectroscopy on the Hobby-Eberly Telescope (HET).
elements.sent-2411-003: Our 13 high confidence candidates (including the six confirmed) have properties consistent with prior known blue blobs and are inconsistent with being low-mass galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-004: Most candidates are concentrated in relatively dense regions, roughly following filamentary structures within the cluster, but avoiding its center.
elements.sent-2411-005: Three of our candidates are likely the stellar counterparts of known `optically dark' clouds of neutral hydrogen in the cluster, while a further four are widely separated extensions to previously known blue blobs.
elements.sent-2411-006: The properties of our new candidates are consistent with previous conclusions that blue blobs likely originated from ram pressure stripping events, however, their locations in velocity--projected cluster-centric radius phase-space imply that their parent galaxies are not on their first infall into the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-007: Through our ongoing follow-up program with HET we aim to confirm additional candidates, however, detailed understanding of the stellar populations and star formation histories of blue blobs will require JWST observations.
elements.sent-2411-008: In galaxy clusters, ram pressure stripping \citep{Gunn+1972 is a ubiquitous process \citep[e.g.][]{Chung+2007,Boselli+2018,Poggianti+2019,Boselli+2022,Roberts+2022 that drives the evolution of infalling star-forming galaxies by pushing out their gas reservoirs and eventually quenching star formation \citep[e.g.][]{Abadi+1999,Vollmer+2001,Solanes+2001,Tonnesen+2007,Crowl+2008,Bahe+2015,Cortese+2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: In the intermediate stage after ram pressure stripping begins, but before quenching, galaxies often exhibit dramatic ``jellyfish" structures with tendrils of gas and star-formation stretching out in their wake \citep[e.g.][]{Kenney+2004,Ramatsoku+2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In some cases, dense clumps of intense star formation are visible, sometimes referred to as ``fireballs" \citep[e.g.][]{Yoshida+2008,Jachym+2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In others, long (10s of kpc) trails of hot, shocked gas can be identified with H$\alpha$ imaging \citep[e.g.][]{Kenney+1999,Yoshida+2002,Boselli+2018b .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Similarly large features are also commonly identified in cluster galaxies from continuum radio synchrotron emission in tails of hot gas \citep[e.g.][]{Gavazzi+1987,Vollmer+2004,Chen+2020 , and \hi radio spectral line emission in those with cool, neutral gas \citep[e.g.][]{Chung+2007,Ramatsoku+2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite the scale of these jellyfish structures (up to $\sim$100~kpc) they are generally connected/associated with a parent galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, recently \citet{Jones2022 identified a new class of objects, referred to as ``blue blobs," that are typically isolated from any galaxy by 100s of kpc.}
elements.sent-2411-015: These objects are actively star-forming, with both UV and H$\alpha$ emission, extremely low stellar mass ($<10^5$~\Msol), made up seemingly entirely of young ($<$200~Myr) stars, metal-rich ($12 + \log \mathrm{O/H > 8.2$), and have radial velocities \citep[$-500 < cz_\odot/\mathrm{km\,s^{-1 < 3000$, e.g.][]{Mei+2007 consistent with Virgo cluster membership \citep{Beccari+2017,Sand+2017,Bellazzini+2018,Jones2022a,Jones2022,Bellazzini+2022 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Given these properties, blue blobs are likely the ram pressure stripping equivalents of tidal dwarf galaxies (TDGs), that is, stellar systems formed out of pre-enriched stripped gas.
elements.sent-2411-017: Such velocities would be difficult or impossible for a TDG to obtain in a tidal interaction \citep{Bournaud+2006 , and these properties therefore point to ram pressure stripping as the likely formation mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This would make them closely related to fireballs and jellyfish, but representing the disconnected extremities of these structures.
elements.sent-2411-019: In most cases \citet{Jones2022 were unable to confidently identify the parent galaxy because of the isolation of the blue blob in question.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The first blue blob discovered was identified serendipitously through a search targeting optically ``dark" compact \hi clouds \citep{Adams+2013 thought to be candidate Local Group dwarf galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, after an optical counterpart of AGC~226067 (also called SECCO~1) was identified \citep{Bellazzini+2015, Sand+2015, Adams+2015 it was quickly realized that this object was far more distant, and likely resides in the Virgo cluster (16.5~Mpc).}
elements.sent-2411-022: Follow-up Hubble Space Telescope (HST) imaging supported this conclusion and showed that its visible stellar population consisted entirely of young stars \citep{Sand+2017 , while integral field spectroscopy with the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) on the Very Large Telescope (VLT) showed that the young stars were at the same radial velocity as the \hi \ gas and that it was surprisingly metal-rich \citep{Beccari+2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: A handful of further examples of blue blobs were subsequently identified through an intensive visual search based on Next Generation Virgo cluster \citep[NGVS,][]{Ferrarese+2012 and Galaxy Evolution Explorer \citep[GALEX,][]{Martin+2005 images \citep{Sand+2017 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Follow-up observations confirmed that these also had properties broadly consistent with SECCO~1 \citep{Jones2022a,Jones2022,Bellazzini+2022,JoneBC6 , indicating that blue blobs are a population of objects, rather than SECCO~1 being a lone enigma.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Identifying and characterizing this population is key to understanding their overall properties and likely formation pathways.
elements.sent-2411-026: Furthermore, they appear to be inconsistent with current simulations of ram pressure stripping in clusters and likely hold important clues for understanding this critical phenomenon \citep[][and references therein]{Jones2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Although several of the initial blue blobs were first identified through their \hi \ line emission, \citet{Jones2022 found that not all blue blobs are \hi-rich and thus an optical/UV search is required to create an unbiased sample.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Unfortunately, these objects are faint, have low surface brightness, and are highly irregular, making them challenging to identify with traditional algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-029: There are also likely too few known to make machine learning a viable option, as the training sample would be inadequate.
elements.sent-2411-030: This leaves visual classification as the primary means to identify them at present.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this paper, we present the results of an extensive Zooniverse\footnote{\url{www.zooniverse.org citizen science search covering the entire Virgo cluster using archival optical and UV imaging that takes advantage of the brighter appearance of blue blobs in $u$-band and UV.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Through this search, we have identified 13 new high confidence blue blob candidates, six of which we have already confirmed with follow-up observations, and 22 lower confidence candidates.
elements.sent-2411-033: In addition, we have identified numerous examples of likely jellyfish structures connected to Virgo member galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the following section, we discuss the search methodology and candidate classification.
elements.sent-2411-035: In \S\ref{sec:HETdata we describe our optical spectroscopy follow-up program.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In \S\ref{sec:starsgas we outline how we estimate the stellar and gas properties of our candidates and in \S\ref{sec:locations we discuss their location within the cluster and associations with known objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In \S\ref{sec:stellarprops \& \S\ref{sec:rpsloc we contrast the properties of our candidates with those of low-mass, star-forming galaxies, and discuss their likely points of origin in general.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Finally, we present our conclusions in \S\ref{sec:conclusions .}
elements.sent-2411-039: In this section we describe the optical and UV images used for the search for blue blobs, outline the search process that volunteers performed, and define how the resulting candidates were classified.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our search for new ``blue blob'' candidates relies on optical images from the NGVS \citep{Ferrarese+2012 , the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey \citep[DECaLS,][]{Dey+2019 and UV images from GALEX \citep{Martin+2005 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: NGVS was a deep survey of the entire Virgo cluster spanning approximately 100~sq~deg (see Figure~\ref{fig:tiles ), performed with the MegaCam 1~sq~deg imager on the Canada-France-Hawaii telescope (CFHT).}
elements.sent-2411-042: Images were taken in $u^\ast griz$ bands between 2009 and 2013, with a typical point source depth of $m_g = 25.9$ mag.
elements.sent-2411-043: The survey footprint is split up into $\sim$1~sq~deg tiles, which are publicly available from the Canadian Astronomy Data Centre\footnote{\url{www.cadc-ccda.hia-iha.nrc-cnrc.gc.ca (CADC).}}
elements.sent-2411-044: In the case of DECaLS and GALEX, we rely on the $gri$ and FUV+NUV mosaics for the DESI (Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument) legacy imaging surveys, which is accessed via the Legacy Viewer\footnote{\url{www.legacysurvey.org/viewer cutout service.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: We used a Python script to iterate through all the NGVS 1~sq~deg tiles (shown as blue boxes in Figure \ref{fig:tiles ) and split them each into 400 overlapping cutouts, approximately 3\arcmin \ across ($512 \times 512$ pixels with a pixel scale of 0.37\arcsec.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The characteristic appearance of blue blobs is that they are faint, very blue, and clumpy.
elements.sent-2411-047: We therefore produced color RGB images of each cutout using the bluest filters ($u^\ast$ and $g$) and the $i$-band (which has more complete coverage over the survey footprint than $r$-band).
elements.sent-2411-048: In particular, using $u^\ast$-band as the blue channel in the RGB image ensures that only the bluest objects appear as blue in the cutouts.
elements.sent-2411-049: In addition to producing the optical RGB cutouts, our script automatically retrieved GALEX NUV+FUV and DECaLS images (with identical fields of view to the NGVS cutouts) from the Legacy Survey viewer.

elements.sent-2411-050: Few blue blobs are currently known, so automated search approaches are poorly suited to their identification.
elements.sent-2411-051: For example, there are too few known objects to train a convolutional neural network.
elements.sent-2411-052: Additionally, blue blobs are distinctive, fairly unique, and varied in shape and would likely pose a challenge for traditional automated algorithms to identify.
elements.sent-2411-053: Blue blobs, like that in Figure~\ref{fig:BC1 HST , can be faint and hard to spot in optical images.}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, they consist of star-forming regions, so they have bright UV emission, making them distinctly identifiable in GALEX images.
elements.sent-2411-055: For these reasons, visual identification is currently the best-suited search method.

elements.sent-2411-056: To streamline the process of identifying candidates by multiple people we used the citizen science platform Zooniverse, which offers a user-friendly interface and automatically tabulates all classifications, making it an efficient tool for projects that involve visual classification of large quantities of data.
elements.sent-2411-057: We began by uploading a subset of the $\sim$150,000 NGVS, DECaLS, and GALEX cutouts, which were then searched by members of our research team.
elements.sent-2411-058: This allowed us to identify additional examples of blue blob candidates, as well as various spurious sources, that could be used as tutorial materials when we opened the project to the public.
elements.sent-2411-059: The “Blobs and Blurs: Extreme Galaxies in Clusters” project\footnote{\url{https://www.zooniverse.org/projects/mike-dot-jones-dot-astro/blobs-and-blurs-extreme-galaxies-in-clusters was launched to Zooniverse in June 2023 to initially search for blue blobs and diffuse galaxies in the Fornax cluster (Mazziotti et al. (in prep.)).}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The search did not reveal any blue blobs in the Fornax Cluster.
elements.sent-2411-061: This might be attributed to the Fornax Cluster being older than the Virgo cluster or having a lower mass.
elements.sent-2411-062: Nonetheless, we used the same framework and uploaded all 150,000 cutouts from the Virgo cluster for citizen scientists to examine.
elements.sent-2411-063: Zooniverse has supported many prominent projects \citep[e.g., Gravity Zoo, Active Asteroids, Galaxy Spy;][]{GZ, AS, Galaxyspy that would be challenging or impossible to complete with any other approach.}
elements.sent-2411-064: For each cutout, volunteers examined a set of NGVS, DECaLS, and GALEX images, all covering the same field of view within a random part of the Virgo cluster.
elements.sent-2411-065: Note that the cutouts were not centered on any pre-selected candidates, but simply tiled the entire NGVS footprint.
elements.sent-2411-066: Volunteers visually inspected these images to identify potential candidates.
elements.sent-2411-067: They were provided a tutorial and a field guide describing what to look for.
elements.sent-2411-068: The tutorial provided information on how to use the Zooniverse interface to identify and collect information.
elements.sent-2411-069: The first section of the field guide presented images of blue blobs in NGVS, DECaLS, and GALEX and described the appearance of blue blobs in the three different image types.
elements.sent-2411-070: Another section provided images of various blue blobs that the team had discovered before the launch.
elements.sent-2411-071: The field guide also provided information on the common artifacts observed in each type of image.
elements.sent-2411-072: The volunteers were told to identify any object with a blue, clumpy, and irregular appearance accompanied by UV emission in GALEX.
elements.sent-2411-073: They were also advised to consult the field guide frequently to better understand the classification they were attempting.
elements.sent-2411-074: After 10 volunteers had inspected the same image, it was retired and was no longer shown to additional volunteers.
elements.sent-2411-075: As most cutouts contained only Virgo cluster galaxies, background galaxies, and foreground stars, an image counted as having been inspected when a volunteer viewed it and then clicked ``Done" (Figure~\ref{fig:zooniverse ), even if no object in the image was marked.}
elements.sent-2411-076: To identify a blue blob candidate (BC), volunteers used the Zooniverse draw tool to form one or multiple rectangular boxes around the suspected blue blob in the image (Figure\,\ref{fig:zooniverse ).}
elements.sent-2411-077: This classification was then stored in the Zooniverse database for our project.
elements.sent-2411-078: The volunteers could also discuss any cutouts they came across with other people on the project (including our science team).
elements.sent-2411-079: These discussion forums were public and acted as additional training resources for volunteers on the project.
elements.sent-2411-080: In addition, the searchers were also tasked with marking any diffuse galaxies they encountered.
elements.sent-2411-081: While blue blobs can be prominent in NGVS and GALEX, they are typically very faint in DECaLS, as seen in the middle cutout of Figure \ref{fig:zooniverse .}
elements.sent-2411-082: We provided volunteers with the DECaLS cutout primarily to aid with the optical identification of diffuse galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-083: However, this study focuses exclusively on blue blobs, with the classification of diffuse galaxies to be covered in a forthcoming publication.

elements.sent-2411-086: From the 150,000 optical and UV images, a total of 13,787 unique blue blob candidates were identified by volunteers; however, to reduce contamination of false positives classified by just 1 or 2 volunteers, we only considered blue blobs classified by at least 3 volunteers.
elements.sent-2411-087: This brought down the number of blue blob candidates to 658.
elements.sent-2411-088: Subsequently, these classifications underwent a ranking procedure conducted by the research team (\S\ref{candidate classification ).}
elements.sent-2411-089: Our main goal is to find unusual and irregular blue objects in the NGVS with corresponding clumps of UV emission in the GALEX.
elements.sent-2411-090: Many of the blue objects classified in the citizen science search often correlate to identifiable galaxies, foreground stars, or an artifact, leading us to exclude them from our candidate list.
elements.sent-2411-091: We visually differentiate false positives from BCs, which in some cases can be subjective.
elements.sent-2411-092: This led us to separate the classifications into four ranks (defined below).
elements.sent-2411-093: Three team members (M.~Jones, S.~Dey, and D.~Sand) independently assigned one of the listed ranks to each of the 658 candidates based on a visual inspection of its NGVS and GALEX images.
elements.sent-2411-094: The majority opinion was taken to assign the rank.
elements.sent-2411-095: In cases of no majority, a re-vote was conducted, with all three members debating and deciding the rank together.
elements.sent-2411-096: The ranking was performed based on the following definitions:.
elements.sent-2411-097: Rank 1 - These are the strongest candidates.
elements.sent-2411-098: They are faint, extended objects that appear clumpy and irregular in optical images.
elements.sent-2411-100: In addition, rank 1 BCs fulfill two key criteria: they are isolated from any galaxies within the Virgo cluster, and they have clear UV emission.
elements.sent-2411-101: The second of these emphasizes that they are centers of ongoing star formation.
elements.sent-2411-102: Figure \ref{fig:BC1 shows an NGVS and GALEX image of a rank 1 BC.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Besides being irregular, it has distinct blue clumps of star-forming region in the NGVS and corresponding bright clumps of UV emission in the GALEX.

Furthermore, it is a possible optical counterpart to a dark \hi cloud discussed in \S\ref{dark}.} \label{fig:BC1}
elements.sent-2411-104: Rank 2 - In comparison to rank 1 BCs, rank 2 objects are less blue and can even appear as bluish-green in the NGVS cutouts.
elements.sent-2411-105: Their structure may be either more compact than rank 1 BCs, or more smooth (less clumpy).
elements.sent-2411-106: However, they are still evident in UV emission, isolated, and irregular.
elements.sent-2411-107: We suspect some fraction of the rank 2 objects may be background galaxies.

It is irregular in shape, and using Legacy Viewer, it was confirmed that this candidate was not associated with any nearby galaxy.} \label{fig:r2}
elements.sent-2411-109: Rank 3 - These candidates have a significant likelihood of being foreground stars or background galaxies, but are not obviously so.
elements.sent-2411-110: Most of these classifications were blue, point-like sources and small diffuse objects with faint or no UV emission.
elements.sent-2411-111: We do not consider these in our analysis as we expect that they are primarily contaminants.
elements.sent-2411-112: Jellyfish - Jellyfish are structures closely related to blue blobs (\S\ref{sec:intro ), likely the result of ram pressure stripped gas forming stars in a galaxy's wake.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Any blue clumps in the immediate vicinity of a galaxy were classified as jellyfish.
elements.sent-2411-114: Many objects classified as rank 1 or rank 2 BCs were subsequently reclassified as jellyfish after the initial rankings when they were inspected using large cutouts and the neighboring galaxy became apparent.
elements.sent-2411-115: False candidates - Many of the objects identified by the Zooniverse volunteers were deemed to be false candidates.
elements.sent-2411-116: These were generally foreground stars, clear background galaxies, or knots of star formation in the outer disks of spiral galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-117: Rank 0 - In addition to these ranks we also kept track of any unexpected object that is not a contaminant but also did not fit into the above classification scheme.
elements.sent-2411-118: We consider them ``out of pocket" candidates and designate them to rank 0.
elements.sent-2411-119: These objects are generally much larger and brighter than blue blobs.
elements.sent-2411-120: We suspect that most are dwarf galaxies being disrupted.

elements.sent-2411-161: Additionally, FUV data is not available for all the blue blobs.
elements.sent-2411-162: For each object, we selected the archival NUV data from GALEX with the longest exposure time.
elements.sent-2411-163: In brief, the process involves measuring flux within background-subtracted elliptical apertures and calculating uncertainties by placing 10,000 circular apertures of the same area across the GALEX tile, excluding the brightest 1\% of pixels.
elements.sent-2411-164: Magnitudes are derived using the conversions of \citet{Morrissey+2007 and corrected for extinction as in \citet{Wyder+2007 .}}
elements.sent-2411-165: These magnitudes are then converted to SFRs following \citet{Iglesias-Paramo+2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-166: The NUV SFR rate for rank 1 and 2 BCs is provided in Table~\ref{table1 .}
elements.sent-2411-167: The Arecibo Legacy Fast ALFA (Arecibo L-band Feed Array) survey, or ALFALFA \citep{ALFALFA , was a low-resolution drift scan survey of the sky to map the \hi content of nearby galaxies ($z<0.06$) using the Arecibo Observatory.}
elements.sent-2411-168: It detected \hi in more than 30,000 low redshift galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-169: The survey also covered all of the Virgo cluster.
elements.sent-2411-170: If detected, \hi spectra of blue blobs provide information about their radial velocity and \hi mass, or an upper limit on the \hi mass if undetected.
elements.sent-2411-171: Using the ALFALFA data cubes, we extracted \hi spectra for our rank 1 and 2 BCs.
elements.sent-2411-172: Each spatial pixel in the ALFALFA data spans 1\arcmin.
elements.sent-2411-173: To extract spectra, we summed the intensity values of all the pixels within a square of length of 7 pixels centered on the pixel containing the target.
elements.sent-2411-174: The sum is then normalized by the beam area.
elements.sent-2411-175: We calculated the root mean square (rms) noise ($\sigma_\mathrm{rms}$) from the spectra, masking the emission due to the Milky Way and any artifacts present in the data.
elements.sent-2411-176: We designate BCs as having a detection and containing neutral hydrogen if they exhibit a distinct \hi emission line with an intensity $\geq$5$\times$rms.
elements.sent-2411-177: We expect any large neutral hydrogen reservoirs associated with blue blobs in the Virgo cluster to have already been detected, as this is a well-surveyed region.
elements.sent-2411-178: Therefore, in addition to extracting our own spectra from the ALFALFA data cubes, we also match the spatial positions of our BCs with objects in existing \hi catalogs of the Virgo cluster, specifically the Arecibo Galaxy Environmental Survey \citep[AGES,][]{RhysTaylorAGES and the ALFALFA extragalactic \hi source catalog \citep{ALFALFAcat .}}
elements.sent-2411-179: We adopt the \hi mass of any \hi source that corresponds to the spatial position of the blue blob.
elements.sent-2411-180: To do this, we search for \hi sources within 2\arcmin~of each blue blob and then confirm with the NASA/IPAC extragalactic database (NED)\footnote{https://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/ whether the \hi source corresponds to the blue blob.}
elements.sent-2411-181: If there is a match in both catalogs, we take the \hi mass given in the AGES catalog as this is the deeper of the two surveys.
elements.sent-2411-182: For blue blobs with no \hi detection and no match, we estimate upper limits for their \hi mass.
elements.sent-2411-183: To do this, we calculate the upper limit on the flux of the blue blob using the equation, \begin{equation}\label{int flux} \int S(V) \, dV < 5 \sigma_\mathrm{rms} \sqrt{\Delta c \Delta v}\end{equation} where $\Delta c$ is the channel width of 5\,\kms and $\Delta v$ is the assumed velocity width of 30\,\kms, which is typical for low-mass objects.
elements.sent-2411-184: We then use this integrated flux (Equation~\ref{int flux ) to obtain the upper limit on \hi mass: \begin{equation}\label{HI mass calc} \frac{M_{\text{HI}}}{M_{\odot}} = 2.356 \times 10^5 D_{\mathrm{Mpc}}^2 \int S(V) \, dV\end{equation} where $D_{\mathrm{Mpc }$ is the distance of the blue blobs (in Mpc) assumed to be within the Virgo cluster at 16.5~Mpc.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: There are 12 blue blobs matched with \hi sources in either catalog.
elements.sent-2411-186: The names and mass values of the corresponding \hi sources, along with the \hi mass limits for the remaining blue blobs, are listed in Table~\ref{table1 .}

elements.subsection-2411-008: Blue blobs associated with HI dark clouds
elements.sent-2411-205: From our search, we have identified four BCs (all rank 1s) that appear to be the optical counterparts of previously known dark \hi clouds in the Virgo cluster.
elements.sent-2411-206: Where possible, we confirmed these associations by matching the H$\alpha$ velocity of the blue blobs from the follow-up observations with HET with the \hi velocity of the clouds.
elements.sent-2411-207: We discuss each of the optical counterparts below.
elements.sent-2411-208: Recently, \citet{JoneBC6 confirmed the discovery of BC1230+0945 as the stellar counterpart to the ALFALFA Virgo 7 cloud complex, which was thought to be optically dark since its discovery in 2007 \citep{Kent+2007 .}}
elements.sent-2411-209: BC1230+0945 lies on the NW tip of the \hi cloud complex.
elements.sent-2411-210: Its \halpha velocity of 500 \kms matches closely with the \hi velocity of 524 \kms for the cloud \citep{Kent+2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-211: It has a gas fraction of $\sim$20,000 $M_\mathrm{HI /M_\ast$ (if the whole \hi cloud complex is considered), making it the most gas-rich stellar system ever discovered.}
elements.sent-2411-212: The position of BC1236+0801 overlaps with AGESVC1 266, an isolated dark \hi cloud in the southern region of the Virgo cluster.
elements.sent-2411-213: The H$\alpha$ velocity of BC1236+0801 is 1671 \kms, and the \hi velocity of the cloud is 1691 \kms, confirming it as the optical counterpart of the isolated dark \hi complex.
elements.sent-2411-214: Based on this association, the gas fraction of BC1236+0801 is $\sim$1100, with a stellar mass of $1.1\times10^4 M_\odot$ (Table~\ref{table1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-215: BC1236+0801 is $\sim$6\,$\arcmin$ ($\sim$30 kpc) away from galaxy MCG+01-32-111, with a similar velocity of 1788 \kms.
elements.sent-2411-216: However, the galaxy has no obvious jellyfish structure and does not seem to have shocked gas, as seen on the Virgo Environmental Survey Tracing Ionised Gas Emission \citep[VESTIGE;][]{BoselliniVestige .}

elements.sent-2411-219: However, this candidate was never followed up with deeper observations, and inspection of the DECaLS and NGVS imaging suggests that it is likely a background galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-220: The location of BC1230+0839 coincides with the dark \hi cloud, AGESVC1 274, also in the southern region of the cluster.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Extensions of previously known blue blobs
elements.sent-2411-232: Four new rank 1 BCs were found in close proximity to previously known blue blobs BC1239+1212, BC1247+1022, BC1226+1423, and BC1227+1510.
elements.sent-2411-233: These new candidates are likely physically associated with the previously known blue blobs, and in some cases, we have confirmed this association through velocity and metallicity measurements.
elements.sent-2411-234: We describe each of the four cases below.
elements.sent-2411-235: We find BC1226+1425 and BC1226+1429 less than 4\arcmin \ (190\,kpc) away from BC1226+1423.
elements.sent-2411-236: BC1226+1429 shares a similar \halpha velocity (-98\,\kms) to BC1226+1423 (-60\,\kms) and BC1227+1510 (-74\,\kms).
elements.sent-2411-237: It also has comparable metallicity, $[\mathrm{O/H ] = -0.05 \pm 0.14$, while BC1226+1423 and BC1227+1510 have $[\mathrm{O/H ] = 0.04 \pm 0.15$ and $0.01 \pm 0.14$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-238: BC1226+1425 does not have any available velocity or metallicity measurements, but based on its (projected) location (Figure~\ref{fig:distBC ), it is also likely a component of the same large structure.}
elements.sent-2411-239: BC1247+1021 appears to be an extension of BC1247+1022, approximately 9~kpc to the southwest in projection.
elements.sent-2411-240: This small clump exhibits an \halpha velocity of 1597~\kms, closely matching BC1247+1022's \halpha velocity of 1584~\kms.
elements.sent-2411-241: Both exhibit nearly identical metallicity values, with $[\mathrm{O/H ] = -0.38 \pm 0.36$ for BC1247+1021 and $-0.40 \pm 0.17$ for BC1247+1022.}
elements.sent-2411-242: BC1247+1021 lies in the same direction as VCC~2037 (relative to BC1247+1022), which \citet{Jones2022a suggested as a possible point of origin, and is 70~kpc to the southeast and at a similar velocity (1507~\kms).}
elements.sent-2411-243: BC1239+1205 is a possible extension of BC1239+1212.
elements.sent-2411-244: It is about 53~kpc southwest of BC1239+1212 in projection.
elements.sent-2411-245: Unfortunately, we have not yet obtained an independent velocity or metallicity measurement, so we cannot confirm this association.

elements.subsection-2411-011: Metallicities of newly confirmed blue blobs
elements.sent-2411-307: Figure 11 of \citet{Jones2022 compares the metallicity of blue blobs with other stellar systems of similar luminosity, such as Local Group and Local Volume dwarfs, tidal dwarf galaxies (TDGs), and extremely metal-poor galaxies (XMPs).}
elements.sent-2411-308: In Figure~\ref{metallicity_plot , we reproduce this plot, but now with double the sample size of confirmed blue blobs with metallicity measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-309: The newly confirmed blue blobs (blue square markers) follow the previous trend and exhibit higher metallicities ($-0.5 \lesssim \mathrm{[M/H] \lesssim 0$) compared to local dwarf galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-310: Their metallicities are similar to TDGs, but they are less luminous and massive.
elements.sent-2411-311: As \citet{Jones2022 argues, for such low-mass stellar systems to have these high metallicities, a viable formation mechanism needs to be capable of explaining the pre-enrichment of their gas.}
elements.sent-2411-312: Gas stripping from a large parent galaxy is a natural explanation for these properties.
elements.sent-2411-313: Furthermore, since the new BCs fall within the metallicity range of previously known BCs, it suggests that they also likely originate from parent galaxies within a similar mass range, $8.3 \lesssim \log M_\ast/\mathrm{M_\odot \lesssim 10.1$ \citep{Jones2022 .}}

elements.section-2411-007: Phase space locations of blue blobs
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{RPS.pdf} \caption{Projected phase-space diagram of blue blobs, jellyfish galaxies, and EVCC galaxies. Rank 1 and rank 2 objects are shown by blue stars and red triangles, respectively. Jellyfish candidates are orange dots, and EVCC galaxies are black circles. We use the same normalizing constants; cluster velocity dispersion ($\sigma_{cl}$) and virial radius ($r_{200}$) as \citet{mun}. We follow the labeling of regions based on the time since the infall of galaxies into the cluster, as shown in \citet{mun}. The projected radius fully covered by NGVS is marked by a solid vertical line. Partial NGVS coverage extending to the Virgo virial radius is shown with blue dashed lines. Part of NGVS coverage extending out to the Virgo B cloud is shown as a dotted line. } \label{fig:RPS}
elements.sent-2411-314: With our current, considerably larger sample of BCs, we are now able to consider the question of their points of origin in a more statistical manner, rather than on a case-by-case basis.
elements.sent-2411-315: Figure 2 of \citet{mun labels the orbital stages of a cluster member galaxy depending on its location in a projected cluster-centric radius versus velocity phase space diagram.}
elements.sent-2411-316: Figure \ref{fig:RPS shows this phase-space diagram for EVCC galaxies, BCs, and jellyfish galaxies with M87 taken as the center of the cluster and the mean radial velocity of 1088 \kms of the M87 subgroup \citep{mun taken as the systemic velocity of the cluster.}}
elements.sent-2411-317: Regions A, B, C, and D in the figure represent different categories of member galaxies designed to approximately trace the time since infall into the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-318: Region A includes ``first infallers," galaxies entering the virial radius of the cluster for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-319: Region B consists of ``recent infallers," galaxies that fell into the cluster within the last 0 to 3.63 Gyr.
elements.sent-2411-320: Region C comprises ``intermediate infallers," which fell into the cluster between 3.63 and 6.45 Gyr ago.
elements.sent-2411-321: Finally, Region D contains ``ancient infallers," galaxies that entered the cluster more than 6.45 Gyr ago \citep{rhee .}
elements.sent-2411-322: Although blue blobs are not galaxies and, therefore, strictly speaking, these phase space regions are not applicable to them, their location within the space may provide clues regarding their parent galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-323: Our BCs are mostly coincident with galaxies in the recent or intermediate infall regions and mostly avoid the ancient infall region.
elements.sent-2411-324: Taken at face value, this suggests that the parent galaxies of blue blobs may have been cluster members for some time rather than on their first infall.
elements.sent-2411-325: However, while ram pressure will do little to decelerate the infall of a parent galaxy (as most of the mass is in dark matter and stars), initially, blue blobs are presumably made entirely of gas and should come to rest with respect to the ICM relatively rapidly.
elements.sent-2411-326: Thus, blue blobs could potentially originate from galaxies in the first infall regions but `sink' in the phase space towards the intermediate regions.
elements.sent-2411-327: Almost no BCs were identified in region A (first infall), which would be a natural result of either scenario described above.
elements.sent-2411-328: However, it should also be noted that much of this region is missing coverage in NGVS and was, therefore, not searched in the Zooniverse project.
elements.sent-2411-329: The lack of BCs in the portions of region A with coverage could also indicate that ram pressure stripping in this part of the cluster is not strong enough to produce blue blobs.
elements.sent-2411-330: Galaxies associated with jellyfish candidates and BCs mostly share the same parameter space; however, jellyfish candidates are slightly more concentrated in the recent infall regions.
elements.sent-2411-331: In Appendix~\ref{sec:phase_map , we re-plot Figure~\ref{fig:distBC but now coloring each EVCC galaxy based on its phase space category.}}
elements.sent-2411-332: BCs also tend to be co-spatial with galaxies in regions B and C of the phase space.
elements.sent-2411-333: Assuming that the separations between blue blobs and their parent galaxies are typically not enormous (e.g., $\lesssim$2$^\circ$ in projection), then this supports the interpretation that the typical parent galaxies are genuinely those residing in the recent/intermediate regions of the infall phase space.
elements.sent-2411-334: One possible way to estimate the distance between a blue blob and its parent galaxy would be to determine how long ago the blue blob began forming stars, which would set a minimum time that it has been separated from its parent object.
elements.sent-2411-335: To determine this will require deep imaging of their resolved stellar populations which would allow their star formation histories to be measured.
elements.sent-2411-336: However, imaging at the requisite depth is only feasible with JWST.
elements.sent-2411-337: Through a citizen science search covering the entire Virgo cluster, we have identified 35 new blue blob candidates in NGVS and GALEX imaging and confirmed 6 of these with emission line spectroscopy with the HET.
elements.sent-2411-338: We separate the blue blob candidates into rank 1 and rank 2 objects, primarily based on their appearance in the optical and UV images (see \S\ref{candidate classification ).}
elements.sent-2411-339: We find that rank 1 blue blob candidates are broadly consistent with previously discovered blue blobs \citep{Jones2022 in terms of their blue colors ($g-i <$ 0), strong UV emission, clumpy morphologies, low stellar mass estimates ($2.5 \lesssim \log{( \frac{M_{* {\mathrm{M_{\odot ) \lesssim 5.5$), low SFR ($-4 \lesssim \log{( \frac{\mathrm{SFR_{NUV {\mathrm{M_{\odot}\,yr^{-1 ) \lesssim -3$), and isolation.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-340: The newly confirmed blue blobs also have velocities consistent with Virgo cluster membership, as well as similarly high metallicities ($0 \lesssim \mathrm{[O/H] \lesssim -0.5$) to previously identified blue blobs \citep{Beccari+2017,Bellazzini+2022,JoneBC6 and TDGs.}}
elements.sent-2411-341: Rank 2 BCs have evident UV emission and similar SFR estimates but are less blue and have smoother morphologies and higher stellar mass estimates than rank 1 BCs.
elements.sent-2411-342: To date we have only observed one rank 2 BC with optical spectroscopy, but no emission lines were detected.
elements.sent-2411-343: Thus, at present, there are no metallicity measurements for rank 2 BCs, and the only velocity measurements come from those that are coincident with known \hi sources.
elements.sent-2411-344: We compared the properties of blue blobs with a sample of low-mass star-forming dwarfs and empirical relations of the star-forming main sequence of galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-345: The combination of stellar masses, SFRs, and metallicities of rank 1 BCs is inconsistent with the galaxies in these comparison samples.
elements.sent-2411-346: A few rank 1 BCs are also amongst the most gas-rich stellar systems ever found, with gas fractions ($M_\mathrm{HI /\mathrm{M_\odot}$) over 1000.}
elements.sent-2411-347: Given these properties, we come to the same conclusion as previous works, that blue blobs are likely isolated star-forming clouds in the Virgo cluster formed as a result of extreme ram-pressure stripping episodes.
elements.sent-2411-348: However, some rank 2 BCs may be false positive candidates and could be galaxies in the background of the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-349: We find a lack of blue blobs in the cluster center and areas with low galaxy density.
elements.sent-2411-350: However, they appear to be more concentrated along the filamentary structures building the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-351: We also confirm the association of three new blue blobs with previously known ``dark" \hi clouds in the cluster \citep{TaylorDark,KentDark .}
elements.sent-2411-352: Based on the positions of blue blobs and jellyfish structures in projected phase space (Figure~\ref{fig:RPS ), we conclude that the parent galaxies of blue blobs have likely been in the cluster for intermediate periods (3.63 to 6.45 Gyr) and are probably not on their first infall.}
elements.sent-2411-353: Virgo was likely the first place that these objects were identified due to its proximity and the large number of surveys that cover it.
elements.sent-2411-354: The Fornax cluster is the next nearest cluster, and given its proximity and the depth of the Fornax Deep Survey \citep[FDS;][]{FDS , it appeared to be the next most viable location for identifying blue blobs.}
elements.sent-2411-355: However, no blue blobs were found through a different citizen science search covering the entirety of Fornax (Mazziotti et al. in prep.).
elements.sent-2411-356: This may be because the Fornax cluster is dynamically older than Virgo or because it is lower mass.
elements.sent-2411-357: Any other cluster would be too distant to achieve the same level of deep coverage as NGVS with current telescopes.
elements.sent-2411-358: Thus, for the immediate future, the Virgo cluster appears to be the only location where these objects can be discovered and studied.
elements.sent-2411-359: The completion of a visual search of such an extensive data set, which otherwise would have been a long and impractical process for a research team, highlights the significant impact and effectiveness of citizen science.
elements.sent-2411-360: Confirmation of additional blue blobs from our candidate list will require further spectral follow-up targeting the \halpha and \hi emission lines.
elements.sent-2411-361: We are actively pursuing these with HET and GBT, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-362: To form a better understanding of when these objects begin forming stars and their subsequent evolution will require deep imaging of their resolved stellar populations, a task which is best-suited to JWST.

elements.sent-2411-384: We present a catalog of 34 new candidate (13 high confidence) isolated, young stellar systems within the Virgo galaxy cluster identified through a citizen science search of public optical and ultraviolet imaging.
elements.sent-2411-385: In the intermediate stage after ram pressure stripping begins, but before quenching, galaxies often exhibit dramatic ``jellyfish" structures with tendrils of gas and star-formation stretching out in their wake \citep[e.g.][]{Kenney+2004,Ramatsoku+2019,George+2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-386: IC3418 in the Virgo cluster presents one of the clearest examples of both traits \citep{Kenney+2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-387: Through this search, we have identified 13 new high confidence blue blob candidates, six of which we have already confirmed with follow-up observations, and 21 lower confidence candidates.
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{NGVSfootprint.pdf} \caption{NGVS tiles (blue boxes) overlaid on the area enclosed by the main Virgo cluster virial radius (green). The NGVS coverage extends towards the Virgo B cloud (in the south), but lacks coverage right at the virial radius in most other regions. Each of these tiles was split into 400 overlapping cutouts for the citizen science search.} \label{fig:tiles}
